preamble: The nasalized low vowel [ã] diphthongizes to [aw] or [au] through the development of a palatal or labial glide. This occurs historically in Romance languages as nasal consonants are lost and the nasalized vowel nucleus develops an off-glide to maintain phonological contrast. The shift is particularly prominent in Portuguese, where three distinct Latin endings (-anum, -anem, -onem) merged into the single nasal diphthong [ɐ̃w̃] by the 14th-15th centuries.

examples.context: Historical development from Galician-Portuguese period; some dialects retain nasal diphthongs
Modern realization of nasal diphthong in -ão endings; pan 'bread' → pão [ˈpɐ̃w̃]

period: Medieval-Modern (14th century onwards); Synchronic (modern Romance dialects)
